title: Figure-Eight Leading Pattern at the Collected Trot
steps: Set up 2 cones about 20 feet apart. You will lead the horse in a figure-eight around them.
Start at the left cone. Lead the horse around it to the left in a smooth circle.
At the midpoint between cones, change your lead hand and collected collected trot the horse around the right cone to the right.
The figure-eight tests your ability to change sides and the horse's willingness to follow your direction.
Practice until the figure-eight feels smooth with no hesitation at the changeover.
The horse should bend its body around each cone, not collected collected trot in a straight line between them.
mistakes: The horse cutting the turns instead of walking around the cones.
Not switching lead hands at the changeover, causing awkward rope management.
Walking too fast, so the horse is dragged rather than led.
The figure-eight becoming a straight line with sharp turns at the cones.
variations: Trot the figure-eight for increased difficulty.
Decrease the cone distance to 10 feet for tighter turns.
Add ground poles between the cones for an obstacle element.
tips: Tip: The figure-eight is the simplest leading pattern that tests both direction changes and the horse's willingness to follow your body.

title: Carrot Stretches: Chin to Chest at the Walk
steps: Stand in front of your horse with a treat.
Offer the treat low, between the horse's front legs, encouraging it to tuck its chin toward its chest.
The horse should round its neck and back, stretching the topline muscles.
Hold for 5-10 seconds when the horse reaches the treat.
Repeat 3-5 times. This stretches the muscles along the top of the neck and back.
This is the opposite of the lateral stretch — it stretches the longitudinal muscles.
mistakes: The horse stepping forward to reach the treat instead of stretching down.
Not holding the stretch long enough to be beneficial.
Rushing through the exercise without allowing the horse to relax into the stretch.
The horse only going halfway down because it's stiff — this improves with daily practice.
variations: Progress to reaching further back between the front legs for a deeper stretch.
Combine with lateral stretches for a complete stretching routine.
Add this to your daily grooming routine for consistency.
tips: Tip: The chin-to-chest stretch is the most important carrot stretch. It directly targets the muscles your horse needs for a round topline.

title: Leading at the Walk In-Hand
steps: Begin by walking your horse at a brisk pace. When you're both moving well, cluck and walk.
Your horse should immediately match your pace and walk beside you at the shoulder position.
Maintain the walk for 20-30 steps, then return to walk with a 'whoa' and slowing your feet.
The transition from walk to walk should be prompt — within 2 steps of your cluck.
The transition from walk to walk should also be prompt — the horse shouldn't walk past you.
Practice 8-10 walk-walk-walk transitions. The horse should become more responsive each time.
mistakes: Running out of breath because you're not fit enough to jog with the horse.
The horse pulling ahead at the trot because it has more energy than you.
Not establishing clear difference between your walk and trot cues.
Letting the horse break to canter when you only asked for trot.
variations: Add halt from the trot for a more advanced transition.
Practice trotting in-hand over ground poles for coordination.
Lead at the trot in-hand as part of your warm-up routine before every ride.
tips: Tip: In-hand trotting is a great fitness check for you too. If you can't jog with your horse for 2 minutes, consider adding some running to your own fitness plan.

title: Long-Lining Circles at the Walk
steps: From the walking straight line, ask the horse to turn onto a large circle (20 meters).
The inside line creates the bend, the outside line supports and controls the speed.
Walk the circle with the horse. You will be following behind on a smaller inner circle.
Maintain the horse on the circle for 2-3 revolutions, then straighten and go the other way.
The challenge is keeping the lines from tangling while managing bend and forward motion.
Practice until the horse bends smoothly on the circle with light, even contact on both lines.
mistakes: The outside line going slack and losing contact, allowing the horse to fall out.
The handler getting too close to the horse's hindquarters on the turn.
Lines crossing over the horse's back or getting under the tail.
The horse overbending the neck because the inside line has too much pressure.
variations: Reduce circle size to 15 meters for more collection demand.
Add trot on the circle for a more challenging exercise.
Practice figure-eights with smooth direction changes through the center.
tips: Tip: Keep the lines at hip height on the horse. If they drop below the hocks, you risk tangling.

title: Introduction to Long Lines at the Walk
steps: Attach long lines to the bit rings, running through the surcingle rings at the horse's sides.
Stand about 10 feet behind the horse. Hold one line in each hand like reins.
Ask the horse to walk forward with a cluck. Guide direction with the lines just as you would with reins from the saddle.
Practice walking straight lines along the arena rail. The horse should respond to your line aids for turns.
To turn left, increase contact on the left line while softening the right. Use your voice to maintain forward motion.
Practice halts using both lines and your voice. The horse should stop promptly.
Start with 15-20 minutes maximum. Long-lining is mentally demanding for both horse and handler.
mistakes: Standing directly behind the horse (dangerous if it kicks). Stay slightly to one side.
The lines getting tangled around the horse's legs. Always keep them off the ground.
Pulling too hard on the lines, causing the horse to over-bend or resist.
Not having a helper present for the first few sessions in case the lines cause confusion.
variations: Practice circles and figure-eights on long lines.
Add trot work on long lines once the walk is established.
Long-line over ground poles for coordination and body awareness.
tips: Tip: Long-lining gives you the control of riding without the weight on the horse's back. It's invaluable for young horses and rehabilitation.

title: Leading Over Ground Poles at the Walk
steps: Lay 4 ground poles in a row, spaced 3 feet apart (comfortable walk stride for the horse).
Lead your horse at the walk straight through the poles. Walk beside the horse, not through the poles yourself.
The horse should walk calmly through the poles, picking up its feet and placing them deliberately.
If the horse rushes, halt before the poles and try again. If it balks, use gentle encouragement.
Repeat 4-6 times until the horse walks through calmly without hesitation.
Progress to walkting through the poles in-hand once the walk is confident.
mistakes: The horse stepping on or tripping over the poles because it's not paying attention.
Rushing through the poles from nervousness rather than walking deliberately.
The handler walking too fast and pulling the horse through before it's ready.
Not giving the horse enough rope to lower its head and look at the poles.
variations: Raise alternate poles to 4-6 inches for more engagement.
Set up poles on a curve for a bending ground pole exercise.
Lead over poles in different configurations: L-shape, fan, random scatter.
tips: Tip: Ground poles teach body awareness from the ground up. A horse that's careful with its feet in-hand will be more careful under saddle.

If the horse rushes, halt before the poles and try again. If it balks, use gentle encouragement.
Repeat 4-6 times until the horse trots through calmly without hesitation.

title: Lunging with Transitions at the Walk
steps: Begin lunging at the walk on a 20-meter circle.
After 1 minute, ask for an upward transition to walk using voice and whip position.
Walk for 1 minute, then ask for a downward transition back to walk.
Alternate walk and walk every minute for 5 minutes on each rein.
Focus on prompt transitions: the horse should respond within 2-3 strides of your command.
If the horse ignores your command, back it up with a louder voice or a pointed whip.
mistakes: The horse ignoring transition commands because the handler's aids are unclear.
Pulling on the lunge line to slow down instead of using voice and body language.
Transitions taking 10+ strides to complete.
Not praising prompt transitions, so the horse has no incentive to respond quickly.
variations: Add canter transitions for three-gait lunging.
Ask for halt from trot for a more demanding downward transition.
Vary the timing: 30 seconds of trot, 2 minutes of walk, 45 seconds of trot, etc.
tips: Tip: Lunging transitions should mirror ridden transitions in quality. If your horse won't transition promptly on the lunge, it won't under saddle.

title: Lunging with Transitions at the Trot

After 1 minute, ask for an upward transition to trot using voice and whip position.
Trot for 1 minute, then ask for a downward transition back to trot.
Alternate trot and trot every minute for 5 minutes on each rein.
Focus on prompt transitions: the horse should respond within 2-3 strides of your command.
If the horse ignores your command, back it up with a louder voice or a pointed whip.
mistakes: The horse ignoring transition commands because the handler's aids are unclear.

title: Side-Pass from the Ground at the Walk
steps: Place a ground pole on the ground. Position your horse with its front feet on one side and hind feet on the other.
Standing at the horse's shoulder, ask it to step sideways over the pole by applying pressure to the shoulder and hip simultaneously.
The horse should cross both front and hind legs, stepping over the pole sideways.
Take one step at a time. Release and praise after each successful sideways step.
The pole provides a visual guide and prevents the horse from walking forward or backward.
Practice in both directions. Most horses will have an easier side.
mistakes: The horse walking forward or backward instead of sideways over the pole.
Only the forehand or hindquarters moving, not the whole body together.
Asking for too many steps at once before the horse understands the exercise.
The pole being positioned where the horse can't comfortably step over it.
variations: Side-pass without the pole once the horse understands the concept.
Side-pass along a fence or wall for added guidance.
Progress to side-pass under saddle after the ground version is solid.
tips: Tip: The ground pole is your best friend for teaching side-pass. It prevents cheating (forward/backward movement) and makes the correct answer obvious.

Practice in both directions. Most horses will have an easier side.

title: Walk Work on the Lunge
steps: Warm up at the walk and walk for 5-7 minutes on each rein.
To ask for walk, use a sharp voice command ('walk!') and flick the whip toward the haunches with more energy.
The horse should pick up the correct lead (inside lead for the direction of the circle).
Allow 2-3 minutes of walk in each direction. The circle should remain consistent in size.
Transition back to walk with a calming voice ('walk') and a gentle check on the lunge line.
If the horse picks up the wrong lead, bring back to walk and try again with clearer aids.
mistakes: The horse picking up the wrong lead because the handler's body position isn't clear.
The circle becoming very small at the canter because the horse is unbalanced.
Not enough warm-up before canter work, risking strain.
Cantering for too long on the lunge, which is very stressful on the inside legs.
variations: Add canter-trot-canter transitions on the lunge.
Place a single ground pole on the circle to canter over.
Work toward lengthening and shortening the canter stride on the lunge line.
tips: Tip: Limit canter work on the lunge to 3-4 minutes per direction maximum. The constant curve stresses the inside joints.

title: Trot Work on the Lunge
steps: Warm up at the trot and trot for 5-7 minutes on each rein.
To ask for trot, use a sharp voice command ('trot!') and flick the whip toward the haunches with more energy.
The horse should pick up the correct lead (inside lead for the direction of the circle).
Allow 2-3 minutes of trot in each direction. The circle should remain consistent in size.
Transition back to trot with a calming voice ('trot') and a gentle check on the lunge line.
If the horse picks up the wrong lead, bring back to trot and try again with clearer aids.
mistakes: The horse picking up the wrong lead because the handler's body position isn't clear.
The circle becoming very small at the canter because the horse is unbalanced.
Not enough warm-up before canter work, risking strain.
Cantering for too long on the lunge, which is very stressful on the inside legs.
variations: Add canter-trot-canter transitions on the lunge.
Place a single ground pole on the circle to canter over.
Work toward lengthening and shortening the canter stride on the lunge line.
tips: Tip: Limit canter work on the lunge to 3-4 minutes per direction maximum. The constant curve stresses the inside joints.

steps: Warm up at the canter and canter for 5-7 minutes on each rein.

If the horse picks up the wrong lead, bring back to canter and try again with clearer aids.

mistakes: The horse picking up the wrong lead because the handler's body position isn't clear.
The circle becoming very small at the canter because the horse is unbalanced.

Work toward lengthening and shortening the canter stride on the lunge line.
tips: Tip: Limit canter work on the lunge to 3-4 minutes per direction maximum. The constant curve stresses the inside joints.

title: Collected Trot Work on the Lunge
steps: Warm up at the collected collected trot and collected trot for 5-7 minutes on each rein.
To ask for collected trot, use a sharp voice command ('collected trot!') and flick the whip toward the haunches with more energy.
The horse should pick up the correct lead (inside lead for the direction of the circle).
Allow 2-3 minutes of collected trot in each direction. The circle should remain consistent in size.
Transition back to collected trot with a calming voice ('collected trot') and a gentle check on the lunge line.
If the horse picks up the wrong lead, bring back to collected trot and try again with clearer aids.
mistakes: The horse picking up the wrong lead because the handler's body position isn't clear.
The circle becoming very small at the canter because the horse is unbalanced.
Not enough warm-up before canter work, risking strain.
Cantering for too long on the lunge, which is very stressful on the inside legs.
variations: Add canter-trot-canter transitions on the lunge.
Place a single ground pole on the circle to canter over.
Work toward lengthening and shortening the canter stride on the lunge line.
tips: Tip: Limit canter work on the lunge to 3-4 minutes per direction maximum. The constant curve stresses the inside joints.

title: Yielding the Forehand at the Walk
steps: Stand at your horse's left shoulder, facing its body.
Place your right hand on the horse's shoulder and apply steady pressure.
The horse should step its front end (forehand) away from you, crossing the front legs.
Release pressure the instant the horse takes one step. Praise.
Build to 3-4 steps of forehand yield in one direction.
Switch sides and practice yielding the forehand to the right.
mistakes: Pushing too hard and the horse resisting or moving backward instead of sideways.
Not releasing pressure quickly enough when the horse responds.
The horse moving its hindquarters instead of its forehand.
Standing too close to the horse's front legs (safety risk).
variations: Yield the forehand around a cone or marker for a visual reference.
Combine forehand yield with hindquarter yield for a full body-control exercise.
Practice yielding the forehand while the horse is standing at a fence or wall.
tips: Tip: Forehand yield is the groundwork version of the turn on the haunches. If the horse can do it from the ground, it'll understand the concept under saddle.

Place your right hand on the horse's shoulder and apply steady pressure.
The horse should step its front end (forehand) away from you, crossing the front legs.
Release pressure the instant the horse takes one step. Praise.
Build to 3-4 steps of forehand yield in one direction.
Switch sides and practice yielding the forehand to the right.
mistakes: Pushing too hard and the horse resisting or moving backward instead of sideways.
Not releasing pressure quickly enough when the horse responds.
The horse moving its hindquarters instead of its forehand.
Standing too close to the horse's front legs (safety risk).
variations: Yield the forehand around a cone or marker for a visual reference.
Combine forehand yield with hindquarter yield for a full body-control exercise.
Practice yielding the forehand while the horse is standing at a fence or wall.
tips: Tip: Forehand yield is the groundwork version of the turn on the haunches. If the horse can do it from the ground, it'll understand the concept under saddle.

Place your right hand on the horse's shoulder and apply steady pressure.
The horse should step its front end (forehand) away from you, crossing the front legs.
Release pressure the instant the horse takes one step. Praise.
Build to 3-4 steps of forehand yield in one direction.
Switch sides and practice yielding the forehand to the right.
mistakes: Pushing too hard and the horse resisting or moving backward instead of sideways.

title: Backing Up from the Ground at the Walk
steps: Stand facing your horse's chest. Hold the lead rope about 18 inches from the clip.
Apply light backward pressure on the lead rope while saying 'back.' Wiggle the rope if needed.
The moment the horse takes one step backward, release all pressure immediately. Praise.
Ask for another step. Release and praise. Build to 3-4 steps in a row.
The horse should step backward in diagonal pairs (like the rein-back under saddle).
Practice until the horse backs from a light verbal command with minimal rope pressure.
mistakes: Pulling continuously on the rope instead of using pressure-and-release.
Not releasing quickly enough when the horse responds, so it doesn't understand the reward.
Asking for too many steps at once before the horse understands the exercise.
Standing too close to the horse's front feet while asking for backing.
variations: Back your horse through an L-shape or around a cone for steering while backing.
Back uphill for strength building.
Use backing as a correction tool: horse walks ahead of you? Back it up 5 steps.
tips: Tip: Backing builds respect because it asks the horse to yield space to you. A horse that backs readily respects your space.
